coredata.dc:title: Okara hydrogel reinforced with P-functionalized carbon quantum dots for soil water retention and phosphorous release
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Jausoro, Juan Sebastián
Foglia, María Lucía
Rigacci, Laura Natalia
Copello, Guillermo Javier
Villanueva, María Emilia
coredata.dc:description: Climate change has intensified drought and desertification, causing severe threats to ecosystems, agriculture, and food security. Developing sustainable solutions to enhance soil water retention and nutrient availability is essential for mitigating these impacts. In this study, hydrogels derived from okara, named POKgel, have been synthesized through soft hydrolysis of okara, incorporating phosphorous-derivatized carbon quantum dots (PCQD) to introduce hydrophilic groups and achieve phosphorus doping, as confirmed by spectroscopic analyses. According to swelling and deswelling analyses, POKgel showed a remarkable water adsorption capacity of 522.5 ± 9.2 %, in contrast to the 158.2 ± 4.6 % exhibited by the untreated hydrogel (OKgel). Soil physical analyses, including particle density, bulk density, and porosity, have indicated that POKgel has enhanced soil structure by reducing bulk density and increasing porosity, leading to better oxygen, water, and fertilizer availability. Plant growth studies revealed that, while initial seed germination rates have been slightly lower in POKgel-treated soils, long-term root development and overall plant performance have been superior to both untreated controls and OKgel-treated soils. The higher phosphorus availability in POKgel-treated soils contributed to these improvements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Okara
Hydrogel
Soil water retention
Waste valorization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: P functionalized carbon quantum dots (PCQD) and POKgel synthesis
Determination of particle density, bulk density, and porosity of the soil
Screening of OKgel and POKgel for plant growth performance under water-limited conditions
Synthesis and characterization of PCQD, OKgel and POKgel
Water holding and retention in sandy soils
P leaching in sandy soil and quantification in sandy soil, gels and wheat
Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. FT-IR spectra of Okara residue, PCQD, OKgel and POKgel. B. FT-Raman spectra of OKgel and POKgel.
Swelling of OKgel and POKgel at different pH values.
Water retention properties of control, POKgel, and OKgel.
Phosphorous concentration in sandy soil, after 5 days of interaction with the hydrogels. * indicates a significant difference from the other treatments according to ANOVA and Tukey’s test (p < 0.05).
Biodegradation of OK gel and POK gel.
Dry weight and root length of wheat.

head.ce:title: Okara hydrogel reinforced with P-functionalized carbon quantum dots for soil water retention and phosphorous release

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Climate change has intensified drought and desertification, causing severe threats to ecosystems, agriculture, and food security. Developing sustainable solutions to enhance soil water retention and nutrient availability is essential for mitigating these impacts. In this study, hydrogels derived from okara, named POKgel, have been synthesized through soft hydrolysis of okara, incorporating phosphorous-derivatized carbon quantum dots (PCQD) to introduce hydrophilic groups and achieve phosphorus doping, as confirmed by spectroscopic analyses. According to swelling and deswelling analyses, POKgel showed a remarkable water adsorption capacity of 522.5 ± 9.2 %, in contrast to the 158.2 ± 4.6 % exhibited by the untreated hydrogel (OKgel). Soil physical analyses, including particle density, bulk density, and porosity, have indicated that POKgel has enhanced soil structure by reducing bulk density and increasing porosity, leading to better oxygen, water, and fertilizer availability. Plant growth studies revealed that, while initial seed germination rates have been slightly lower in POKgel-treated soils, long-term root development and overall plant performance have been superior to both untreated controls and OKgel-treated soils. The higher phosphorus availability in POKgel-treated soils contributed to these improvements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change exacerbates drought and desertification worldwide, creating unfavorable conditions for many ecosystems and communities [1,2]. Rising temperatures and erratic precipitation patterns have led to more frequent and severe droughts, leaving vast areas of once-fertile land barren. Desertification—the transformation of fertile soil and vegetation into arid, desert-like landscapes—is advancing at an alarming rate [3]. This climate change-induced drought and desertification threatens food security and displaces communities, leading to resource conflicts and migration [4,5]. Urgent and coordinated global action is essential to mitigate these impacts, including sustainable land management practices and ambitious emissions reductions.

Hydrogels are emerging as a promising solution in agriculture to combat the challenges caused by drought [6–8]. These water-absorbent polymer materials can retain and release moisture efficiently in the root zone of crops. When incorporated into the soil, hydrogels can help improve water retention, reducing the required frequency and volume of irrigation [7]. Therefore, farmers can optimize their water usage and contribute to more sustainable and efficient agricultural practices, mitigating the adverse effects of drought while promoting increased crop yields and food security. In addition, hydrogels can also release fertilizers and/or herbicides in a controlled manner or as a medium for inoculating nitrogen-fixing bacteria [7,8].

There are few commercially available hydrogel products capable of absorbing and retaining water molecules for use in agriculture (Aqualic ™ (sodium acrylate and acrylic acid), Luquasorb ™ (Potassium acrylate and acrylic acid crosslinked with 0.3 % Pentaerythritol triallyl ether), Stockosorb ™ (Acrylamide and sodium/ potassium acrylate), Krilium ™ (Copolymer consisting of vinyl acetate and maleic anhydride (VAMA)), PUSA hydrogel (Cellulose-graft-anionic polyacrylate superabsorbent polymer), Alsta hydrogel (Potassium polyacrylate) [8]. Despite their agricultural benefits, the synthetic nature of hydrogels has environmental concerns [9]. The production and disposal of synthetic hydrogels contribute to environmental pollution and resource depletion since the manufacturing processes often involve the use of non-renewable resources and the release of harmful by-products [10]. Additionally, the persistence of synthetic hydrogels varies depending on their composition and environmental conditions. Studies have shown that polyacrylamide and polyacrylate-based hydrogels exhibit slow degradation rates in soil and aqueous environments [11].

Okara, a byproduct of soy milk and tofu production, is a nutrient-rich material with a composition that varies depending on its origin and processing. Typically, it contains approximately 9–10 % moisture, 21 % protein, 55 % dietary fiber, 1.5 % ash, and 13–14 % fats and oils [12]. Although okara has recognized applications in animal feed and agriculture, it remains frequently underutilized and discarded, especially in small-scale production systems. Several materials have been synthesized using okara, demonstrating its versatility as a sustainable resource. For instance, okara has been combined with acrylic acid and acrylamide to develop bio-based hydrogels for moisture retention for agricultural applications. Additionally, dried okara has been employed in the fabrication of adsorbents for water purification, effectively removing organic pollutants. In the food industry, okara has been incorporated into functional foods and biodegradable packaging materials [13–18]. This approach exemplifies the concept of a circular economy, where waste is transformed into valuable resources, promoting sustainability and reducing the environmental footprint of various industries [19,20].

Carbon quantum dots are nanoscale carbon-based materials that exhibit exceptional optical and electronic properties [21,22], making them highly versatile in various applications, such as electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions [23,24] or energy harvesting applications [25]. When doped with phosphorus, they exhibit higher hygroscopicity than their non-derivatized counterparts [26,27]. This enhanced water retention is attributed to the introduction of phosphorous groups, which contribute to improved surface interactions with water molecules. Besides, phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plant development, playing a key role in ensuring optimal growth and yield in wheat crops [28]. Wheat needs this element during its growth stages. Therefore, fortifying soil with an adequate supply of phosphorus is usually done to meet the nutritional needs of wheat plants and optimize their productivity [29].

This work describes the development of an okara hydrogel to mitigate the impact of drought on wheat crops. The primary objectives of this study were: (1) to develop an environmentally friendly hydrogel from okara, an agro-industrial byproduct, using a sustainable synthesis approach, (2) to enhance the hydrogel’s swelling properties and phosphorus-loading capacity by incorporating PCQDs, (3) to characterize the physicochemical properties of the hydrogel and evaluate its stability and functionality in soil, (4) to assess the impact of the hydrogel on wheat growth under drought conditions, with a focus on water retention and nutrient delivery.

By integrating waste valorization with targeted phosphorous delivery, this approach offers a dual benefit: it provides a sustainable solution for repurposing food industry waste while addressing key agricultural challenges. The hydrogel’s ability to enhance soil moisture retention and improve phosphorus bioavailability presents a promising strategy for increasing crop resilience to drought, reducing reliance on conventional fertilizers, and promoting more sustainable farming practices. Given the increasing frequency of extreme weather events and the need for efficient resource utilization in agriculture, the proposed system represents a practical and scalable solution for improving soil health and supporting global food security.

Peanuts and soybeans were purchased from a local store. The soil employed in this study was prepared using 20 % of organic residues from a deciduous forest, characterized by a high content of mull-type organic matter, combined with 80 % of sand. Wheat seeds (Triticum aestivum), were kindly provided by Molino Argentino. Glycerol, NaOH, and H3PO4 were acquired from Biopack (Buenos Aires, Argentina), and filter paper grade 42 was purchased from Whatman (Kent, England).

Soybeans were soaked in water overnight and then milled and filtered. The resulting residue was the okara we used, which was dried and milled once again. The okara hydrogel (OKgel) was prepared by mixing 3 g of okara and 2 g of glycerol with a 0.5 M NaOH solution in ethanol: water (1:1) and was stirred at 37°C for 2 h. After that, the mixture was placed in a mold and let dry at 37°C for 18 h. The dried block was washed three times with deionized water and let dry again in the same conditions. The dried blocks were conserved in a flask until they were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: P functionalized carbon quantum dots (PCQD) and POKgel synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: PCQD were prepared following the hydrothermal method [30]. Three grams of milled peanut shells and 10.0 mL of 1 M H3PO4 were carbonized at 220°C for 2 h. The resulting carbonized product was dispersed in deionized water, filtered with a filter paper (Whatman grade 42), and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was dialyzed for 24 hours in distilled water. The determination of dissolved solids showed the amount of quantum dot material present in the sample, having 1.33 % in the solid state and a phosphorous concentration of 3.34 g/L. The OKgel was swelled using a PCQD suspension and dried at 45°C to obtain POKgel. The same procedure was performed using a 3.0 g/L P solution and used as a control in swelling studies.

Attenuated total reflectance Fourier Transform InfraRed (ATR-FTIR) spectra (diamond attenuated total reflectance) of okara residue, PCQD, OKgel and POKgel, were recorded using a Nicolet iS50 Advanced Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded with 32 scans and a resolution of 1 cm−1. FT-Raman spectra were acquired with an excitation laser beam of 1064 nm, 0.25 W laser power, resolution of 4 cm−1, 400 scans. All samples were previously dried for 24 h at 60 °C to avoid water-related bands interference. Optical microscopy images were obtained using a Zeiss Axioplan epifluorescence microscope equipped with an HBO 50 W lamp and a filter set for blue light, green light, and UV excitation. The samples were examined at a magnification of × 400.

The points of zero charge (PZC values) of the hydrogels were determined by the pH drift method [31]. A series of solutions of different pH values were prepared using a solution of 0.01 M NaCl that had been boiled to remove dissolved CO2 and then cooled to room temperature in completely filled containers, which were tightly sealed to prevent CO₂ from redissolving. The pH was adjusted to a value between 1 and 10 using 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH, and their pH was recorded (initial pH). Then, the samples were immersed in each solution, and the drift in the pH after 48 h was measured (final pH). Since the addition of a solid into a solution (with a particular pH) induces a shift in the pH in the direction of the PZC, then the pH at which the addition of the sample did not induce a shift in the pH was taken as the PZC. All measurements were carried out in triplicates.

The swelling behavior of the materials was assessed according to Villanueva et al. [32]. Briefly, 0.0500 ± 0.0020 g of OKgel and POKgel were equilibrated in 10 mM phosphate solutions ranging from pH 4–8. After equilibrium was reached, the hydrogels were removed from the solution and accurately weighed. The swelling was calculated following Eq. 1.
(1)
Swelling
%
=
W
f
−
W
0
W
0
100
where W
f is the weight of the equilibrated gels and W
0 is the initial weight of the gels.

Water holding and water retention of sandy soil with OKgel and POKgel were measured using the method reported by Zhu et al. [15]. For water holding measurements, W
s grams (approximately 20 g) of sandy soil supplemented with 5 % (w/w) of OKgel or POKgel powders were carefully placed into a pot with 3 holes. The bottom holes of the pot had been previously sealed with filter paper and weighed (defined as W
0 ). Samples in the pots were soaked in tap water for 1 day. After that time, the excess water at the bottom and outer wall of the pots was removed with tissue paper. The pots were weighed (defined as W
1 ). At the same time, the control treatment without gel was also carried out. Based on W
0 and W
1 , the value of water holding capacity in the potting mix was calculated according to Eq. 2.
(2)
W
h
%
=
W
f
−
W
0
W
s
100

The study of the water retention capacity of sandy soil with gels immediately followed the above procedures. Throughout the experiment, the treatments were maintained at room temperature, and weighed every two days for 10 days (defined as W
t ). The dry weight after it reached a constant weight was defined as W
dry . The value of water retention (W
r ) was calculated according to Eq. 3.
(3)
W
r
% =
t
W
t
−
W
dry
W
1

W
dry
100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of particle density, bulk density, and porosity of the soil
ce:section.ce:para:
Soil particle density (PD) was assessed using a standard procedure [33]. Initially, the empty pycnometer was weighed, then filled with sandy soil and reweighed. Subsequently, the pycnometer was filled with water, and the overall weight of the pycnometer with soil and water was recorded. Finally, the pycnometer was filled solely with water and weighed again. PD of the soil was calculated by using the following Eq. 4:
(4)
PD =
δ
w
w
s −
W
a
( W
s
−
W
a
) − (
W
sw
−
W
w
)
where ბ
w is the density of water, W
s is the weight of pycnometer including dry soil sample, W
a is the weight of empty pycnometer, W
sw is the weight of pycnometer filled with soil and water, and W
w is the weight of pycnometer filled with water only.

Bulk density (BD) of the sandy soil and the samples was determined based on the method described by Bora et al. [33]. For this, samples were prepared using OKgel and POKgel by weighing 5 g of the gels and 95 g of sandy soil. After the preparation of the samples, 30 mL of tap water was added to each cup, and they were kept at room temperature for 3 days. The air-dried soil was further dried in an oven at 105°C. Finally, BD was determined from the mass and volume of the soil (Eq. 5).
(5)
BD =
M
V
where M is the mass of dry soil and V is the total volume of soil.

Further, the porosity of the soil was calculated based on the values of PD and BD by using the following equation (Eq. 6).
(6)
Porosity
%
= [ 1 −
BD
PD
]
where BD and PD are bulk density and particle density of soil, respectively.

Germination efficiency in presence of the gel was determined according to Uysal et al. [34]. Briefly, wheat (Triticum aestivum) seeds were sowed in pots containing the prepared sandy soil additivated with 5 % of OKgel and POKgel. A control was performed. A total of 20 seeds were sowed in each pot and three of such pots were prepared for each treatment. Seeds were considered germinated upon radicle emergence. The percentage of seeds germinated was determined according to the following equation (Eq. 7):
(7)
% germination =
germinated seeds
total seeds
100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of OKgel and POKgel for plant growth performance under water-limited conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments were conducted using OKgel and POKgel to assess their effects on wheat growth performance. The hydrogels were mixed with the previously prepared sandy soil at a concentration of 5 % (w/w) before being transferred into pots. The control group consisted of sandy soil without hydrogels. Water was added to fully saturate the sandy soil prior to seed sowing. After 21 days, the plants were harvested, and above-ground growth performance was evaluated.

In order to evaluate how much phosphorus was released into the soil, 100 g of sandy soil and 5 g of OKgel or POKgel, previously hydrated with distilled water, were weighed. A control without the addition of hydrogel was also prepared. After 5 days, the soil was sieved to separate the hydrogel. Subsequently, around 2.0000 ± 0.1000 g of sieved soil sample was weighed into tubes, and 14.00 mL of Kurtz and Bray extractant solution (NH4F 0.03 M in HCl 0.025 M) was added. The tubes were capped and shaken on a reciprocal shaker for 5 minutes. After this time, the solution was filtered on paper and measured using the Murphy and Riley method [35].

Labile phosphorous leaching was studied using glass columns, which were filled with the previously described sandy soil and kept under indoor incubation. For the column's settlement, glass columns of 1.7 cm in internal diameter and 25 cm long were used. A layer of cotton was placed at the bottom of the columns, and 50 g of dry and sieved sandy soil was added to each one. After that, distilled water was added until saturation. Then, 0.1000 ± 0.0020 g of POKgel and OKgel were added on the top of the column, and 10.0 mL of distilled water was added every single day for 15 days. A control without gel was also prepared. The average amount of solution collected per application was 9.0 mL per column. The leached solution was collected and measured for its P content using the Murphy and Riley method. The wheat obtained in 2.6 and OKgel and POKgel were dried and weighed. The samples were carbonized using HNO3 34.7 % as oxidant at 550 °C for 5 h. The samples were dissolved with an HNO3 0.347 % and measured spectrophotometrically using Murphy and Riley method. P in PCQD solution was also quantified using the same method.

Biodegradation was studied following the method described by Dutta et al. [36]. A definite amount of OKgel and POKgel was placed in a pot with sandy soil. Three sets of samples for each gel were prepared in this way. Before burying the samples, tap water was added to each pot to make the soil pasty. The test was carried out for 45 days and water was sprayed every alternate day to keep the soil moisture level constant. The samples were taken out one by one at a certain time interval (15, 30 and 45 days) for testing their biodegradation. Samples were rinsed with distilled water and dried in a hot air oven at 50 °C until a constant weight was obtained. The weight of the dried samples was measured and the percent weight loss was calculated by using Eq. 8.
(8)
weight loss
%
=
W
i
−
W
f
W
i
100
where Wi and Wf are the initial and final weights of the sample before and after biodegradation test.

All experiments and their corresponding measurements were conducted at least in triplicate under identical conditions and statistically analyzed by one-way ANOVA and Tukey comparison post-test if ANOVA p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PCQD, OKgel and POKgel
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of the PCQDs used in this study was previously reported by our research group [30]. In brief, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of PCQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior, with the maximum emission wavelength shifting in response to excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm. The highest PL intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm, with an emission peak at 366 nm. This behavior was commonly attributed to factors such as size distribution, surface functional groups, structural defects, and the quantum confinement effect.

The okara hydrogels, OKgel and POKgel, were obtained via soft hydrolysis, making effective use of the hole residue. Consequently, these hydrogels consisted of proteins, carbohydrates, fats, and retained elements from the original okara residue. Evidence of the presence of these compounds can be observed in the FT-IR spectra of the Okara, OKgel, and POKgel in Fig. 1A. Within the Okara spectrum, the bands observed at 2927 and 2850 cm−1 accounted for the presence of CH2 groups within hydrocarbons, and 1744 cm−1 corresponded to the presence of CO stretch. These CH2 and carbonyl groups were usually found in lipids and fatty acids. The presence of C–H stretching also indicated the presence of cellulose polysaccharides. The peaks near the 3282 cm−1 indicated the presence of O–H stretching vibration of the polymer and hydrogen bonding, which probably showed the existence of cellulose and hemicellulose. The 1637 and 1044 cm−1 spectral lines are characteristic of glucuronic acid and C-O bonds, respectively. These bonds were present in cellulose and hemicellulose [37–39].

Comparing the POKgel and OKgel spectra, no significant differences were observed. However, they exhibited variations when compared to the original Okara spectrum [40]. Notably, the bands at 2927 and 2850 cm−1 were less intense in both hydrogels than in the Okara residue, suggesting alterations in the hydrocarbon composition. Furthermore, the absence of the 1744 cm−1 band in the hydrogel spectra indicated changes in the lipid and fatty acid content during the hydrogel formation process.

The spectrum of the PCQD was quite different; broad bands were found at 856 cm−1, 954 cm−1, corresponding to P-O-C stretching, 1098 cm−1, corresponding to P-O stretching, and 2290 cm−1, corresponding to P-H stretching [26,41]. These hydrophilic groups were in congruence with the high hygroscopicity and confirmed phosphorus doping in PCQD.

Fig. 1B shows the OKgel and POKgel FT-Raman spectra. The observed bands were similar in both spectra, and the interpretation could be found elsewhere [42]. Briefly, the band observed at 1654 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the presence of CO stretching vibrations in proteins and lignin, while the band at 1459 cm⁻¹ was attributed to CH₂ and CH₃ bending modes of aliphatic moieties of okara components. The bands at 1303 cm⁻¹ and 1259 cm⁻¹ were associated with aromatic ring vibrations and C-O stretching in polysaccharides and lignin. Additionally, the band at 1071 cm⁻¹ is associated with C-O-C vibrations in cellulose and hemicellulose. The different bands confirmed the presence of oil, carbohydrates and proteins in the okara, while the shifting of 1459 cm⁻¹ would be related to different protein conformation due to denaturation during gel synthesis [43,44].

Figure SM1 shows the microscopic images of OKgel and POKgel, both exhibiting a rough and heterogeneous surface. The images under a UV filter revealed no significant differences in fluorescence between OKgel and POKgel, despite the expected fluorescence in POKgel due to the incorporation of PCQD. This lack of distinction was attributed to the intrinsic fluorescence of OKgel, which may mask the expected emission from PCQD in POKgel.

Initially, the swelling behavior of OKgel treated with a 3.0 g/L phosphorus solution, OKgel, and POKgel was evaluated. The results showed that the swelling of POKgel was significantly higher compared to the other two cases (Okgel: 158.2 ± 4.6, OKgel treated with P solution: 171. 1 ± 8.2 POKgel: 522.5 ± 9.2, p < 0.05). This finding demonstrated the superior performance of POKgel, probably due to the incorporation of phosphorus-containing groups during its synthesis, enhancing its hydrophilic and pH-responsive properties. Besides, the functional groups on PCQDs may interact with the polymer network, increasing electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding within the hydrogel which improved swelling behavior. Since PCQD were incorporated into the gel after gelation, no strong interactions were intentionally promoted. Nevertheless, probably, the hydrophilic surface of PCQD would interact with hydrophilic moieties of proteins and polysaccharides chains in Okara, allowing more water to enter to the pores, which otherwise will remain interacting with each other stabilizing a less swelled structure. The PO₄3- solution, being purely ionic, did not contribute to such structural enhancements. Based on these observations, subsequent studies focused on comparing POKgel and OKgel.

The behavior of the hydrogels at different pH values was studied using PZC measurements and swelling/deswelling assays. The PZC analysis showed values of 5.4 for OKgel and 2.7 for POKgel. A comparison of swelling capacities between OKgel and POKgel ( Fig. 2) at pH 3, 7, and 11 revealed that POKgel exhibited significantly higher water absorption in all tested cases (p < 0.05).

The improved swelling of POKgel can be attributed to its phosphorus-containing groups, such as phosphates, which are highly hydrophilic [45]. These groups formed strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules, facilitating greater water absorption and retention [46]. Additionally, the low PZC value of POKgel indicated the presence of negative charges on the hydrogel surface starting from pH 2.7. This negative charge resulted in electrostatic repulsion between the polymer chains, contributing to an expanded network structure that enhances swelling [47].

Interestingly, POKgel demonstrated reduced water absorption at pH 3, with a swelling capacity approximately 250 % lower than that observed at pH 7 and 11, probably due to a lower negative charge presence. This behavior suggested that POKgel is more suitable for use in neutral to alkaline environments. In this study, the soil pH was 6.8, which optimized water retention and minimized surface evaporation.

In contrast, OKgel, with a PZC value of 5.4, exhibited a lower capacity to form negative charges. This reduced charge diminishes electrostatic repulsion within the hydrogel network, resulting in a decreased ability to absorb water. Additionally, the differences in swelling for OKgel across pH 3, 7, and 11 were not statistically significant (p > 0.05).

Fig. 3 shows the water retention behavior of the various hydrogels under two treatments at different temperatures, 20°C and 30°C. In Fig. 3, it was observed that the OKgel lost a higher percentage of water in both treatments compared to the POKgel.

Considering the results obtained from the observations of swelling and deswelling, we could expect better performance for the POKgel, improving the water retention capacity of the soils, increasing the water content available for plants, and in some cases, reducing the need for irrigation, thus decreasing the impact on the water cycle caused by the irrigation systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water holding and retention in sandy soils
ce:section.ce:para: The water holding capacity was 179.4 ± 2.7 % for the treatment with POKgel, 172.4 ± 0.9 % for OKgel, and 166.2 ± 2.9 % for the control. Sandy soil supplemented with POKgel showed improved water holding capacity compared to the sandy soil treated with OKgel and the sandy soil. The performance of POKgel-treated soil was significantly different from the OKgel-treated and the untreated soil (p > 0.05), proving that the treatment with POKgel enhanced the soil's ability to retain water. Fig. 4 shows that POKgel exhibited superior water retention properties compared to OKgel in sandy soils. The increased water-holding capacity of POKgel translated into reduced irrigation frequency and improved soil moisture availability for plant roots. POKgel and OKgel are hydrogels designed to enhance water retention in soil. While both hydrogels shared certain properties, such as their gel-like structure, the presence of PCQD in the POKgel changed the mechanisms through which they retain water. Since sandy soils presented poor water retention, leading to frequent irrigation requirements and increased susceptibility to drought stress, the incorporation of hydrogels acted as water reservoirs, mitigating water loss and creating a more favorable environment for plant growth.

The PD of the soil was 2.49 ± 0.01 g/cm3. The obtained BD for the untreated soil was 2.21 ± 0.01 g/cm3, whereas soil treated with OKgel and POKgel was 0.996 ± 0.004 and 1.112 ± 0.007 g/cm3, respectively. According to other studies, the introduction of the hydrogel enhanced the development of larger pores within the soil [48,49]. Consequently, the BD of the treated soil was reduced in comparison to the untreated soil. This phenomenon can be attributed to the dehydration of hydrogel particles from their expanded state, resulting in the creation of empty spaces between the soil particles [33]. The soil treated with OKgel and POKgel exhibited the lowest value, and the highest value was noticed for untreated soil.

Porosity values of the treated and untreated soil were also calculated, and the results were: untreated soil: 11.4 %, OKgel treated soil: 60.1 % and POKgel treated soil: 55.0 %. From these results, it was observed that the porosity of the soil increased with the decrease in BD value. This was because the lower density defines a greater volume for the same weight. Therefore, from the lower BD and higher porosity of treated soil, it can be said that OKgel and POKgel can provide good health to the soil by increasing oxygen as well as fertilizer supplies along with water and can be used for plant growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: P leaching in sandy soil and quantification in sandy soil, gels and wheat
ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorus concentration in the gels was 99.38 ± 0.04 mg/kg for OKgel and 190.72 ± 0.09 mg/kg for POKgel. Fig. 5 shows the P concentration in the soil after interacting with the gels for 5 days. The P concentration in the soil treated with POKgel was significantly higher than in the control. The concentration of phosphorus in soil treated with POKgel stands out as a significant advantage for its use as a hydrogel in agriculture. Fig. 6 shows the daily accumulated leached labile P (soluble P), representing the soluble phosphorus movement through the sandy soil. The figure illustrates the release of labile P in solution over time (in days) for three conditions: a control sample, OKgel, and POKgel. The control and OKgel exhibited a slow and steady release of P, with values remaining below 10 mg/L throughout the experiment. In contrast, POKgel showed a significantly higher P release, reaching approximately 60 ppm by day 15. Although, P amount in POKgel almost doubled the OKgel one, the release P leaching of the former is 6 times the latter. This suggested that the incorporation of PCQD into the hydrogel matrix enhanced the release of labile P, which could be beneficial for applications requiring sustained phosphorus availability in soil.

Scheme 1 illustrates the proposed mechanism of phosphorus (P) leaching. Initially, the gels existed as dry blocks with PCQD embedded within them. Upon contact with water, they swelled until they reached their maximum water uptake. As the environment became drier, water gradually diffused into the soil, carrying P along with it. Additionally, the gels may undergo biodegradation, gradually releasing the P stored within their structure.

Phosphorous concentration in wheat was also determined. Wheat grown in OKgel-treated sandy soil had 9.09 ± 0.07 mg/g, the one treated with POKgel 9.12 ± 0.03 mg/g and the control 8.93 ± 0.17 mg/g. No significant differences were found among treatments, probably because the soil already had high concentrations of P. Differences in macroscopic characteristics such as root length were found and described in the following section.

The germination of wheat seeds was evaluated in the three studied cases. It was observed that the germination at three days in the treated soil was slightly lower (76.6 ± 15.3 % for OKgel and 80.0 ± 17.3 % for POKgel) than in the untreated soil case (96.6 ± 5.7 %). This slight inhibition when a hydrogel is incorporated to the soil was also observed in other studies [15]. Hydrogels can retain a large amount of water, which could create an excessively moist environment for seeds during germination. Excess moisture may reduce the oxygen available in the soil, which is crucial for seed respiration and early development.

After 21 days of wheat plant growth, they were harvested. The dry weight and the root length were registered and presented in Table 1. No statistically significant differences were observed in dry weight among treatments (p > 0.05). However, the length of the roots obtained after the POKgel treatment was significantly higher than when treated with OKgel and in the control. This can also be observed in Figure SM2. The observed difference in the performance of the soil treated with POK gel, as compared to the other treatments, can be attributed to the higher P concentration in the soil. Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plant growth, and its efficient acquisition is crucial for various physiological processes, including root development and photosynthesis. The presence of phosphorus-functionalized carbon quantum dots in the soil treatment probably facilitated increased phosphorus availability to the plants, promoting more robust root growth and overall plant development.

The biodegradation of the hydrogels using the soil burial method was also studied. This method was widely used to study the biodegradation of polymeric materials due to its simplicity and its close resemblance to real-world waste disposal conditions simulating a highly challenging environment where complex mixtures of natural microbial flora were present. In our case, Fig. 7 shows the % of degradation of OK gel and POK gel achieved approximately 75 % in 45 days, proving the biodegradability of the materials.

Upon reviewing the literature, several studies on hydrogel-based slow-release fertilizers can be found. For instance, Dong et al. [50] reported the synthesis of a slow-release fertilizer prepared by one-step free radical copolymerization of starch phosphate carbamate and acrylamide, with and without urea addition (SPC). Priya et al. [51] developed a biodegradable hydrogel composed of urea-loaded cellulose nanofibers and carboxymethyl cellulose (UCNF) as a slow-release, plant growth-promoting fertilizer. Similarly, Ahmad et al. [52] designed an eco-friendly, controlled-release fertilizer based on a cellulose hydrogel derived from wastepaper, using epichlorohydrin as a crosslinker and carboxymethyl cellulose as a gelling agent.

Table 2 presents a comparative analysis of the key characteristics of these hydrogels. Compared to other hydrogel-based fertilizers, POKgel offers a multifunctional approach by combining high water retention, controlled phosphorus release, and improvements in soil structure. A major advantage of POKgel is its complete valorization of agricultural waste, ensuring sustainable resource utilization while avoiding potentially harmful reagents such as acrylamide or epichlorohydrin.

The utilization of hydrogels, particularly POKgel, presents a promising solution for addressing water retention challenges in sandy soils. Besides, although POKgel has been primarily designed for phosphorus release, its structure and composition suggest potential applicability for other essential nutrients such as nitrogen or potassium. The multifaceted benefits, including improved water retention, controlled nutrient release, and positive effects on soil structure and plant growth, underscore the potential of POKgel for sustainable agricultural practices.

Maria Villanueva: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Guillermo Copello: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Laura Rigacci: Validation, Investigation. Maria Foglia: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Juan Jausoro: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the author(s) used ChatGPT-3.5 as a translation assistant and to improve language and readability. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported with grants from Universidad Nacional de Luján (PI^2+) and Agencia Nacional de Promoción Científica y Tecnológica (PICT-2018-01731).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.116837.
